# **SIFLY Inc. Smart Gym Management System** 

*Software Requirements Specification*

*SRS Version 2.0*

Team T05  
14 April 2026 

Liepa Lavickyte (manager)  
Finn Ellis  
Samuel Landis  
Ike Osode  
Younes Slaoui

 

 

 

 

  CS 460 Software Engineering

# 

# 1\. Introduction  {#1.-introduction}

The objective of the document is to outline the software requirements, which govern the design and implementation of the Smart Gym Management System (SGMS) software. Developed by SIFLY Inc., this software is engineered to resolve the critical safety gaps inherent in modern fitness centers. Traditional gyms are often reliant on member self-sufficiency, leaving individuals vulnerable to unattended injuries, health events, or environmental hazards. The SGMS addresses these operational blind spots by providing a comprehensive, automated safety net that ensures the security of every person on the premises at all times \[1\] \[2\].

The requirements outlined in the document will be fulfilled by the implementation of the SGMS software, which will effectively manage the interfaces between the various hardware components and the Data & Analytics Engine, the centralized processing hub responsible for real-time safety analysis, event classification, and automated alert dispatch. This integration ensures comprehensive oversight by aggregating multimodal data from a network of facility-mounted cameras , environmental sensors continuously tracking temperature, humidity, and air quality (CO2, VOCs, and particulate matter), and wearable biometric wristbands that monitor real-time heart rate. These inputs are processed by a cloud-based, third-party Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) service, Gemini 3.1 Pro. This processing via MLLM will identify health and safety risks such as detected falls or movement anomalies while dispatching immediate and verifiable alerts to on-duty staff \[4\]. To ensure system reliability, the software enforces a human-in-the-loop requirement where all MLLM-generated outputs must be accompanied by raw source data for manual cross-verification.

In addition, the SGMS supports facility optimization through the synthesis of occupancy and equipment usage data into routine management reports. While the system provides personalized monitoring and adjusted alert thresholds for higher-risk individuals, it is legally constrained to operate as a safety and logistics tool, strictly prohibiting the diagnosis of medical conditions or prescription of treatments. The software also maintains strict privacy and regulatory compliance, utilizing an opt-in or opt-out consent model for all biometric and personalized tracking \[3\].

The software specifications of the SGMS are outlined in five sections. Section 1 introduces the purpose; Section 2 provides a general description; Section 3 defines specific requirements and control logic; Section 4 outlines design constraints; and Section 5 defines terms.

# 2\. General Description {#2.-general-description}

The SGMS is a sophisticated data analysis platform designed to transform massive volumes of multimodal inputs into actionable, user-friendly outputs. By replacing manual oversight with an automated network of hardware and software, the system provides two primary functional deliverables: immediate, verifiable alerts and comprehensive routine reports. These alerts, categorized by severity, are always paired with raw source data to ensure human-in-the-loop accuracy, while the routine reports synthesize camera, wearable, and environmental telemetry into hourly, daily, and monthly summaries. Furthermore, the system enables personalized health support by adjusting safety alert thresholds based on voluntarily disclosed member health profiles, ensuring that higher-risk individuals receive tailored monitoring.

The system serves a diverse demographic of users, ranging from casual gym members to higher-risk individuals who benefit from optional, consent-based biometric monitoring. On-duty staff interact with the system via a mobile application to receive high-priority push notifications for quick safety intervention and to manage facility configurations, adjust alert thresholds, and review the detailed analytics required to inform operational decisions and equipment optimization.

The SGMS operates through handling a variety of device inputs and transforming them into the correct deterministic output states. This means all physical hardware elements of the system, from the sensors to the cameras, require their own device controllers and device drivers in order to operate. Figure 1 represents the bridge between the physical world of hardware elements and the software elements of the system.

![][image1]

*Figure 1: SGMS Systems Block Diagram*

The input controllers enable communication with their respective devices, and their data is fed directly into the SGMS. Wearable wristbands and Air Quality sensors transmit heart rate and air quality telemetry directly to the system, while visual data is passed to an MLLM Processing Interface (Gemini 3.1 Pro) to perform injury detection without the need for dedicated machine-mounted sensors. Gemini 3.1 pro is a state-of-the-art multimodal model allowing inputs up to a million tokens and outputs up to \~64k tokens \[5\]. It also supports structured output, leading to more reliable and easy-to-parse responses \[5\]. This fully allows all processing required by the SGMS. Implementation of the SGMS is predicated on the operational assumption that MLLMs are complex, "black box" components that may produce erroneous information, thereby mandating strict human cross-verification of all AI findings before action is taken. It is important to note this makes the system dependent on the reliability of commercial API providers and the facility's network bandwidth to maintain the high throughput required for real-time monitoring.

The system processes these parallel streams, which displays information to staff via the Gym Management Portal. This allows staff to respond to alerts and safety risks across the gym. This management app relies on the internal data stores to perform actions like querying data, manage health profiles, and display reports. This app will be available on web and mobile (required for notifications) and acts as the central interface for human interaction with the system.

# 

# 3\. Specific Requirements {#3.-specific-requirements}

This section describes the specific requirements of the SGMS, including the system interfaces, inputs, outputs, and interactions between the MLLM interfaces and external hardware components. These requirements define how the system monitors the state of the gym and processes the inputs and delegates and confirms outputs.

## 3.1 Use Cases {#3.1-use-cases}

These use cases describe how end-users will interact with the Smart Gym Management System in various conditions representing the capabilities defined in the SGMS RDD Version 2.0.

### **Use Case 1: Critical Safety Event Detection** {#use-case-1:-critical-safety-event-detection}

**Capabilities Represented:** Critical Safety Event Detection

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Primary Actor** | On-Duty Staff |
| **Secondary Actors** | Gym Member, Facility-Mounted Cameras |
| **Goal in Context** | To rapidly identify a member in physical distress and receive a verifiable alert so staff can respond immediately in person. |
| **Preconditions** | Cameras are active and cover all functional gym zones. At least one staff member is on duty with the portal app open on their device. |
| **Trigger** | A gym member experiences a physical emergency (such as a fall) within a monitored zone. |

**Scenario:**

1. A member is exercising in the free-weight zone and falls to the floor.  
2. Staff on duty receive an immediate Critical push notification on their devices flagging the incident, with a short video clip of the event attached for review.  
3. Staff view the video clip to confirm what occurred before physically responding.  
4. Upon confirmation, staff proceed directly to the free-weight zone to assist the member or call emergency services as needed.  
5. The event and staff response are logged in the system for record-keeping.

**Exceptions:**

* *False positive (e.g., a dropped weight misread as a fall):* Staff view the attached clip, determine no emergency occurred, and dismiss the alert in the app.  
* *No staff acknowledge the alert within a defined timeout:* The alert is automatically escalated to Gym Management.  
* *Camera view is partially obstructed:* If the affected member is wearing a biometric wristband and has opted in, any abnormal physiological readings from the device are factored into the alert to help staff assess the situation.  
* *Staff are repeatedly receiving false Critical alerts from a specific zone:* Staff may temporarily suppress camera-based monitoring for that zone in the app until the source of the false positives is resolved.

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Frequency of Use** | Rare / Occasional;  triggered only during emergencies |
| **Channel to Actor** | Gym Management Portal App (push notification) |

---

### 

### **Use Case 2: Environmental Monitoring and Hazard Detection** {#use-case-2:-environmental-monitoring-and-hazard-detection}

**Capabilities Represented:** Environmental Monitoring and Hazard Detection

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Primary Actor** | On-Duty Staff |
| **Secondary Actors** | Gym Management, Environmental Sensors |
| **Goal in Context** | To be alerted when environmental conditions in the facility exceed safe or comfortable levels so staff can investigate and take corrective action. |
| **Preconditions** | Environmental sensors are installed, calibrated, and actively streaming readings. Comfort and safety thresholds are defined in the system. The portal app is accessible to on-duty staff. |
| **Trigger** | A sensor detects a reading such as a rise in CO2, temperature, or VOCs that exceeds a defined threshold. |

**Scenario:**

1. A sensor detects that CO2 or temperature levels have risen above the defined comfort threshold but remain within safe limits.  
2. Staff on duty receive a Warning alert on their devices indicating which zone is affected, with the current sensor readings attached.  
3. Staff review the readings in the app and proceed to the flagged zone to assess conditions.  
4. Based on what they find, staff manually adjust the zone's climate control settings or contact the facility's HVAC service to correct the issue.  
5. The event and the readings that triggered it are logged for later review in management reports.

**Exceptions:**

* *CO2 or temperature levels spike to hazardous, legally unsafe limits:* The event is immediately escalated to Critical and all on-duty staff receive a push alert with the sensor data attached, prompting evacuation or emergency ventilation of the affected area.  
* *A sensor goes offline or stops transmitting:* The system flags the sensor as inactive and surfaces a maintenance notice in the Management Portal, but does not generate a false environmental alert.  
* *A sensor continuously produces erroneous or implausible readings:* Staff or management may snooze or temporarily disable the sensor in the portal until it is serviced or replaced.

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Frequency of Use** | Continuous monitoring; Warning alerts occasional; Critical alerts rare |
| **Channel to Actor** | Gym Management Portal App (push notification for warnings and critical alerts) |

**Open Issues:**

* Precise numerical thresholds for Informational, Warning, and Critical environmental events are yet to be defined.  
* Possible future integration with the facility's HVAC system for automated corrections is under consideration.

---

### 

### **Use Case 3: Biometric Wearable Data Processing** {#use-case-3:-biometric-wearable-data-processing}

**Capabilities Represented:** Biometric Wearable Data Processing

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Primary Actor** | Gym Member |
| **Secondary Actors** | On-Duty Staff, Biometric Wristband |
| **Goal in Context** | For a member to voluntarily enroll in biometric monitoring for a session, enabling the system to support their safety while they exercise, and to cleanly exit that monitoring when their session ends. |
| **Preconditions** | Biometric wristbands are available at the facility. The member has a profile in the system. Staff are present at the front desk to issue and register the device. |
| **Trigger** | A gym member arrives and is offered a biometric wristband for their session, or proactively requests one. |

**Scenario:**

1. A member arrives at the gym and is offered a biometric wristband at the front desk by staff. Alternatively, the member requests one.  
2. The member reviews and signs the biometric monitoring consent form. They may also voluntarily share relevant health information (such as a heart condition) to allow the system to personalize safety thresholds for their session.  
3. If health information is provided, the member reviews the suggested thresholds and chooses to accept or reject them.  
4. Staff register the wristband to the member's profile for the duration of their session.  
5. The member puts on the wristband and proceeds into the gym. Throughout their workout, the wristband monitors their heart rate activity in the background without any required input from the member.  
6. The member finishes their session and returns the wristband to the front desk staff.  
7. Staff deregister the wristband from the member's profile (preferably automatic upon device charge). Biometric monitoring for the member ends and the session data is retained only as needed for reporting or any alerts generated during the session.

**Exceptions:**

* *Member declines the wristband or consent:* No biometric data is collected. The member continues their session and is still covered by camera and environmental monitoring.  
* *Member removes the wristband during their session:* Monitoring stops immediately and the session is closed out from the biometric tracking perspective; no alert is generated solely from the removal.  
* *Wristband fails to sync or loses the member's profile mid-session:* Staff are notified of the device issue via the portal; the member's biometric monitoring is considered inactive until the issue is resolved.

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **When Available** | When biometric wristband opt-in is available |
| **Frequency of Use** | Per session, for members who opt in |
| **Channel to Actor** | In-person at front desk; wristband device during session |

---

### 

### **Use Case 4: Warning Health Event Detection** {#use-case-4:-warning-health-event-detection}

**Capabilities Represented:** Warning Health Event Detection

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Primary Actor** | On-Duty Staff |
| **Secondary Actors** | Opted-In Gym Member, Biometric Wristband |
| **Goal in Context** | To receive a timely, data-backed warning when a member shows unusual physiological signals, so staff can conduct a non-medical wellness check before the situation worsens. |
| **Preconditions** | The member has been issued a biometric wristband, consented to biometric monitoring for the session, and their health profile and personalized alert thresholds are on file. |
| **Trigger** | The member's wristband detects a sustained heart rate reading that breaches their personalized threshold during exercise. |

**Scenario:**

1. A member arrives at the gym and is offered a biometric wristband by front desk staff. The member accepts and provides consent; the wristband is linked to the member's profile for the duration of their session.  
2. The member exercises normally throughout their session while the wristband continuously monitors their heart rate.  
3. During exercise, the member's heart rate sustains an unusual trend that exceeds their personalized threshold.  
4. Staff on duty receive a Warning notification on their devices with the member's heart rate trend and their relevant health profile attached for context.  
5. Staff review the alert and the member's profile. If the reading appears explainable given the member's current activity or health history, staff dismiss the alert in the app.  
6. If the reading is concerning, staff approach the member for a non-medical wellness check to confirm they are feeling well.

**Exceptions:**

* *The wristband loses skin contact or produces a noisy reading:* The system flags the reading as unreliable; staff are not notified of a false health event.  
* *The member removes the wristband or opts out mid-session:* Biometric monitoring for that member stops immediately and no further alerts are generated.  
* *Heart rate reaches an extreme level indicating immediate danger:* The event is escalated from Warning to Critical and all on-duty staff are alerted simultaneously.

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **When Available** | When biometric wristband opt-in is available |
| **Frequency of Use** | Continuous monitoring; alerts triggered occasionally |
| **Channel to Actor** | Gym Management Portal App (push notification) |

**Open Issues:**

* Exact definition of an abnormal "heart rate trend" (e.g., percentage over maximum heart rate sustained for a defined number of seconds) is still to be determined.

---

### 

### **Use Case 5: Facility Data Visibility and Archival Logging** {#use-case-5:-facility-data-visibility-and-archival-logging}

**Capabilities Represented:** Facility Data Visibility and Archival Logging

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Primary Actor** | On-Duty Staff |
| **Secondary Actors** | Gym Management |
| **Goal in Context** | To look up historical system activity (such as a past alert, sensor data log, or usage records) to cross-reference against something occurring in the present and make a more informed operational decision. |
| **Preconditions** | The staff member is logged into the Gym Management Portal. Historical data for the relevant time period exists in the archive. |
| **Trigger** | A staff member encounters a current situation (such as a recurring alert or a member complaint) and wants to verify whether it has happened before or retrieve prior context. |

**Scenario:**

1. A staff member notices a pattern or situation during their shift (for example, a zone is triggering repeated equipment alerts) and wants to check whether this has occurred previously.  
2. The staff member opens the Gym Management Portal on their device and navigates to the activity archive.  
3. The staff member searches or filters the archive by zone, event type, time range, or alert severity to locate the relevant records.  
4. The staff member reviews the historical alerts, sensor readings, or usage logs associated with the situation, including any source data such as video clips or sensor charts that were attached at the time.  
5. With the historical context in hand, the staff member is able to confirm whether the current situation is a known recurring issue, a new development, or a false positive, and responds accordingly.

**Exceptions:**

* *The relevant records fall outside the system's retention window and have been purged:* The staff member is shown a clear indication that no data exists for that period and may escalate the inquiry to management if needed.  
* *The staff member does not have access permissions for certain data categories:* The portal displays only the records within their access level and indicates that additional records require elevated permissions.

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Frequency of Use** | On-demand; as needed by staff during their shift |
| **Channel to Actor** | Gym Management Portal App |

---

### 

### **Use Case 6: Observational Report Generation** {#use-case-6:-observational-report-generation}

**Capabilities Represented:** Observational Report Generation, Real-Time Area Monitoring, Occupancy Tracking, Equipment Usage Tracking

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Primary Actor** | Gym Management |
| **Secondary Actors** | On-Duty Staff |
| **Goal in Context** | To review a structured report of facility activity over time in order to identify recurring safety concerns, underutilized equipment, peak usage patterns, and member behavior that may require management action. |
| **Preconditions** | The management user is logged into the Gym Management Portal. At least one full reporting period has elapsed and a report has been generated for that period. |
| **Trigger** | A report period ends (such as the close of a week) and Gym Management logs in to review the generated report. |

**Scenario:**

1. At the end of the week, a Gym Management user logs into the Gym Management Portal and navigates to the analytics and reports section.  
2. The management user opens the latest weekly report, which presents a summary of zone occupancy trends, equipment usage frequency, peak and off-peak traffic periods, and any recurring alerts or flagged events from the week.  
3. The management user reviews the occupancy patterns to identify which zones are consistently crowded or underused across the week and at what times.  
4. The management user checks equipment utilization data to see which machines are heavily used and which have been largely idle, informing potential decisions about equipment reallocation or removal.  
5. The management user reviews the recurring alerts section to identify whether any safety events (such as environmental warnings or health alerts) have been happening repeatedly in the same zone, suggesting a systemic issue that warrants attention.  
6. The management user identifies any members flagged across multiple incidents during the period and determines whether any follow-up is warranted.  
7. Based on their findings, the management user makes or schedules operational decisions, such as adjusting staffing during peak hours, requesting equipment maintenance, or arranging an HVAC inspection for a repeatedly flagged zone.

**Exceptions:**

* *A zone's occupancy or equipment data is incomplete for the period due to a camera obstruction or outage:* The report clearly flags the gap in data for that zone and indicates reduced confidence in those specific figures, while still presenting the available data.  
* *Management wants to investigate a specific flagged event further:* The management user can navigate from the report directly into the facility data archive (see Use Case 5\) to retrieve the raw records, video clips, or sensor logs associated with that event.  
* *No significant trends or anomalies are present in the report:* The report confirms baseline normal operation for the period and is archived without requiring any management action.

| Field | Detail |
| ----: | :---- |
| **Frequency of Use** | Hourly overviews, daily summaries, weekly reports, monthly reports |
| **Channel to Actor** | Gym Management Portal |

## 

## 3.2 External Interfaces   {#3.2-external-interfaces}

To ensure that the gym facility operates as intended, the SGMS must interact with multiple hardware and software interfaces. These external devices notify the central software regarding member health status, environmental safety, and zone occupancy. In response, the SGMS generates deterministic outputs, which include severity-coded alerts and routine reports, enabling the successful operation of a monitored gym environment. While data stores are part of the SGMS, they are treated as read/write devices for clarity of functional structure.

### 

### 3.2.1 Hardware & Third Party Services {#3.2.1-hardware-&-third-party-services}

This section defines the precise design boundaries between the SGMS control software and the external physical hardware and third-party services it manages.

| External Interface | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Cameras | Facilitates the continuous streaming of visual feeds from gym zones. |
| Environmental Sensors | Facilitates the periodic reading of ambient gym environmental conditions (Air Quality, Temperature, Humidity). |
| Biometric Wristbands | Facilitates the wireless reception of biometric signals from active member wristbands. |
| External MLLM (Gemini 3.1 Pro) | Evaluates camera video feed and responds with requested analyses |

### 

### 3.2.2 Input Events {#3.2.2-input-events}

This section maps the sources of incoming data across the external boundaries to their exact signals, data types, and associated system inputs. Continuous streams are treated as system data events that must be handled.

| Input Source | Associated System Inputs |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Cameras** | \- Continuous video feed stream |
| **Environmental Sensors** | \- Environmental quality alert (if limits exceeded) \- Routine reporting cycle |
| **Biometric Wearables** | \- Biometric data stream \- Band issued to member \- Band removed / returned |
| **MLLM Response** | \- MLLM detected alerts\- Occupancy count updated\- Machine usage logged |
| **Data Stores** | \- Member health profile requested \- Historical gym state retrieved \- Archived report or alert view requested \- Report being generated |
| **Portal App** | \- Makes Gym State and Reports accessible to Gym Staff, Management \- Allows Gym Staff to view and optionally dismiss alerts \- Allows Gym Staff to access health records |

### 3.2.3 Output Actions {#3.2.3-output-actions}

This section maps the destinations of outgoing system data across the external boundaries to their resulting actions.

| Output Destination | Resulting Action |
| :---- | :---- |
| **External MLLM** | Sends video footage and context to the external MLLM AI for: \- Hazard detection\- Injury detection\- Occupancy counting \- Equipment usage analysis |
| **Portal App (Alerts)** | Triggers Alert Push Notifications on staff devices. |
| **Data Stores (Logs & Reports)** | \- Archives Routine Reports and Alert Reports \- Saves new member information |
| **Data Stores (Video Archive)** | \- Saves flagged video clips\- Executes secure deletion of expired video archives |

##  

## 3.3 Control Logic {#3.3-control-logic}

This section defines the deterministic rules and decision logic that govern how the SGMS ingests, evaluates, routes, and acts upon data in real time. This section is not meant to define specific software components, but rather how information is input, processed, and output as it flows through the system. The system is modelled after a four-phase execution loop: Ingestion of Data from Hardware & Devices, Processing & Artifact Creation, Routing, and finally Human Interaction. Specifically, the “Data & Analytics Engine” is a broad term for the components of the system that handle all data processing from inputs to outputs. Figure 2 visually depicts this as a flow across the System Logic Overview Diagram. Specific component state flow is formalized across six component descriptions and their relevant accompanying OMT dynamic diagrams (Figures 3–5).![][image2]

*Figure 2: SGMS System Logic Overview Diagram. Represents flow from inputs to outputs.*

### 3.3.1 Component Descriptions {#3.3.1-component-descriptions}

**Cameras**

Implements hardware communication with cameras and makes their video feeds accessible to software components. The camera feed is then processed by the MLLM, depicted below.

| Inputs | Outputs |
| :---- | :---- |
| *Camera Hardware* | Camera Feed |

**Environmental Sensor**  
Implements hardware communication with environmental sensors and exposes protocols for reading the specified outputs. On initialization, the subsystem enters Idle Monitoring, where it continuously samples sensors, logs readings, and compares them against configured limits, as seen in Figure 3\. Under the \[Normal\] condition the system remains in Idle Monitoring, while \[Warning threshold\] and \[Critical threshold\] conditions transition it to the Warning State or Critical Alert State respectively. The Warning State logs the event and flags the affected zone on the staff dashboard, and can either resolve back to Idle Monitoring or escalate to Critical when conditions worsen. The Critical Alert State dispatches an alert to all staff with sensor data attached, and returns to Idle Monitoring once the alert is acknowledged.

![][image3]

***Figure 3: Environmental Sensor Monitor OMT Diagram***

*This diagram models the environmental sensor data cycle, logging every reading to the data stores before evaluating it against warning and critical thresholds.*

| Inputs | Outputs |
| :---- | :---- |
| *Environmental Sensor Hardware* | Environmental Data Air Quality Temperature Humidity Readings |

**Biometric Wristbands**  
Implements hardware communication with the wireless biometric wristbands. Handles connectivity and provides interfaces for other parts of the software to read the biometric data from each wristband. Once a wristband is paired to a member's profile, the device enters Active Monitoring, where it streams biometric data and compares readings against the member's personalized thresholds, as seen in Figure 4\. Under a \[Normal reading\] the device remains in Active Monitoring, while a \[Warning threshold\] breach transitions it to the Warning Alert state, dispatching a notification to staff with the heart rate trend and health profile. If the heart rate reaches an \[Extreme heart rate\] level, the state escalates to Critical Alert and all on-duty staff are notified simultaneously with the heart rate log. When the member returns the wristband, the device transitions to the Disconnected state, ends the session, retains data for reporting, and resets for reuse.

![][image4]

***Figure 4: Biometric Wristbands OMT Diagram***

*This diagram models the full wristband device lifecycle from front-desk checkout and member pairing through active heart rate monitoring to session disconnect.*

| Inputs | Outputs |
| :---- | :---- |
| *Biometric Wristbands Hardware* | Biometric Data\- Heart rate |

**MLLM Handler**  
Acts as processor of camera feeds and receiver of MLLM classifications. Handles monitoring of occupancy counts for each camera feed. Additionally, it performs injury and hazard detection via Gemini 3.1 Pro. The MLLM Handler verifies the format of results from the MLLM and passes them to the Data & Analytics Engine. Once cameras are activated and zones configured, the subsystem enters Active Streaming, where video feeds are passed to the MLLM handler for frame-by-frame analysis, as seen in Figure 5\. Each Frame Analyzed event routes to Occupancy Tracking, where individuals are counted by zone and logged to the database; the count then feeds Evaluate Capacity, which either returns to Active Streaming under the \[Within limit\] condition or triggers an Overcrowding Alert when \[Over capacity\]. In parallel, an Injury Detected event routes to Event Classification, where severity is classified and a video clip attached; \[Informational\] events route to Log Event for report archival, while Warning or Critical events route to Dispatch Alert to notify staff. Both paths return to Active Streaming once their respective events are logged or dispatched.

![][image5]

***Figure 5: MLLM Handler OMT Diagram***

*This diagram models the continuous video surveillance loop, branching on each analyzed frame into occupancy counting with overcrowding alerts and injury event classification with severity-based routing.*

| Inputs | Outputs |
| :---- | :---- |
| Camera Feed | \- Occupancy Count (by zone) \- Critical Safety Event Detection \- Equipment Usage \- Flagged Video Clips |

**Data Stores**

Various data are collected, stored, and read by the SGMS.

* **Member Health Profiles**  
  Stores member-disclosed information about health, custom heart rate thresholds, etc.

* **Gym States Archive**  
  Stores historical, periodic logs of the gym state for usage and analysis in the SGMS.

* **Reports Archive**  
  Stores archived routine reporting for access by gym management.

* **Video Clips Archive**  
  Stores archived video clips where abnormalities were detected for usage in reporting and alerts.

* **Alert Log**  
  Stores logged alert information for querying by the mobile app and handles new logged alerts

* **Gym State**  
  Tracks current gym state including Occupancy Counts, Current Air Quality, and Current Alert State

**Gym Management Portal App**

User-facing application allowing gym staff to interact with the SGMS. Visualizes and organizes data from the various data stores. Handles emergency alerting and provides staff response controls. Available on both mobile devices and web browsers. The app will allow gym staff to:

- Receive alert push notifications on mobile devices and:  
  - View associated alert data for human verification  
  - Optionally dismiss the alert  
- View data visualizations from the all of the various data stores including:  
  - Alert log  
  - Member health profiles  
  - Generated Reports  
  - Gym States History  
- View dashboards with graphs of relevant metrics such as:  
  - Gym State data trends including usage, occupancy, and air quality  
  - Visual history & quick overview of alerts

# 

# 

# 4\. Design Constraints {#4.-design-constraints}

Many exterior factors have been considered in the design of the SGMS. These constraints ensure the system remains legally compliant, operationally reliable, and physically durable within a gym environment.

## 4.1 Operational Constraints {#4.1-operational-constraints}

The operational requirements impose strict performance standards to ensure member safety and system trust.

* **Real-Time Responsiveness:** The system must process multimodal inputs and deliver Critical Alerts to staff devices within 3 seconds of an incident detection.  
* **Human-in-the-Loop:** No Warning or Critical alert shall be displayed to staff without the accompanying raw source data (video clip or heart rate log). The system is constrained to assist, not replace, human judgment.  
* **Consent Model Degradation:** The software must support both "Opt-In" and "Opt-Out" models. For non-consenting members, the system must strictly utilize anonymized skeleton tracking, where identity-linked biometric data is purged before the MLLM processing layer.

## 4.2 Technical and Network Constraints {#4.2-technical-and-network-constraints}

The architecture must operate within defined technical limitations to ensure stability and security.

* **Bandwidth Management:** To prevent network congestion, the system must use local edge processing to filter static video frames, only uploading "active" video buffers to the cloud-based MLLM.  
* **API Rate Limits and Processing Costs:** Utilizing advanced commercial MLLMs incurs per-token API costs and is at the mercy of provider rate limits. The system architecture is constrained by the need to optimize data payloads and reduce total API cost.  
* **Cybersecurity:** All physiological data and health profiles must be encrypted with AES-256. Video archives must be automatically purged every 30 days to comply with applicable biometric and data privacy regulations, including the Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA), the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), and the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), unless flagged for a legal hold.  
* **Uptime:** To ensure safety coverage remains consistent the SGMS shall maintain an operational uptime of 99.9% during all facility hours as defined by gym management, providing continuous, automated oversight across the entire 4,000 square foot floor plan—including the cardio deck, free weight zone, and machine area .

# 

# 5\. Definition of Terms {#5.-definition-of-terms}

* **Alert Routing Service:** An internal software component that is responsible for packaging alerts with necessary artifacts and directing them to the correct user interfaces based on severity and escalation protocols.  
* **Smart Gym Management System (SGMS):** The integrated hardware and software system responsible for monitoring gym availability, enhancing user experience, and processing data within the gym.  
* **Environmental Sensors:** Hardware devices deployed throughout the facility to continuously monitor temperature, humidity, and air quality.  
* **Air Quality:** A composite measurement of airborne conditions including CO₂ concentration, particulate matter, and volatile organic compounds captured by environmental sensors.  
* **Wearable Biometric Devices:** Gym-provided devices issued to members at entry that capture real-time physiological signals (only heart rate for initial release) during a session.  
* **Immediate Alerts:** Time-sensitive notifications delivered to on-duty staff through the Gym Management Portal App when a Warning- or Critical-level event is detected, always accompanied by raw source data for verification.  
* **Alert Levels:** A three-tier classification system (Informational, Warning, and Critical) used to categorize detected events by severity and determine the appropriate staff notification response.  
* **Data & Analytics Engine:** Orchestrates and unifies data streams, alerts, handles calls to the MLLM interface. Processes and produces routine reports.  
* **Routine Reports:** Periodic data summaries generated at hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly intervals that synthesize camera, wearable, and sensor data into visualizations for management review.  
* **Gym Management Portal App:** The primary staff- and management-facing interface, accessible via a web-based dashboard and iOS/Android app, used for receiving alerts and accessing reports.  
* **Higher-Risk Individuals:** Members who have voluntarily disclosed health conditions or personal circumstances that warrant closer, non-medical monitoring with tailored alert thresholds.  
* **Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM):** A generative AI model capable of processing multiple data types simultaneously, such as video, images, and sensor readings, whose outputs are treated as unverified until cross-checked with raw source data.  
* **Camera Feeds:** Video streams from facility-mounted cameras used for occupancy tracking, fall detection, equipment usage analysis, and anonymized movement monitoring.  
* **Member:** A registered individual with an active gym membership who may use facility equipment and optionally participate in biometric monitoring via the provided wearable device.  
* **Member Data Service:** An internal data management component that stores and retrieves member health profiles to provide context to Data & Analytics Engine.  
* **Management:** Gym administrators who access routine reports, analytics dashboards, and system configuration through the management website to inform facility-level decisions.  
* **On-Duty Staff:** Any trainer or staff member currently working a shift and actively receiving push notifications from the SGMS alerting system.  
* **Opt-In Model:** A consent configuration in which members must explicitly sign a waiver to activate personalized biometric monitoring and identity-linked tracking (the default model).  
* **Opt-Out Model:** An alternative consent configuration in which personalized monitoring is enabled for all members upon enrollment, with the option to decline at any time.


# 

# 6\. Conclusion {#6.-conclusion}

The Smart Gym Management System (SGMS) detailed in this document represents an innovative solution to the operational and safety challenges faced by modern fitness facilities. By integrating automated environmental sensors, wearable biometric devices, and camera-based detection powered by multimodal large language models (MLLMs), the system ensures the continuous monitoring of members' safety and well-being as well as the overall environmental condition in the gym. The human-in-the-loop verification protocol for all MLLM-generated alerts and the flexible "Opt-In" and "Opt-Out" consent models guarantee the reliability of safety interventions while strictly upholding member privacy and regulatory compliance. Implementation of this specification will result in a scalable, data-driven environment that minimizes operational blind spots, provides personalized member support, and maximizes facility utility through actionable long-term reporting.

#
